html:
Viomi Technology (VIOT): A Tiny Profitable ADR Priced Like a Melting Ice Cube

VIOT is not the clean recurring-consumables story the old pitch implied. It is better described as a post-divestiture China home-water supplier with extreme Xiaomi concentration, a credible but still early overseas option, and a stock price that has collapsed to roughly 3x earnings despite a balance sheet that almost covers the entire market cap in liquid assets after all liabilities.

The current ADS price is about $0.98. With each ADS representing three ordinary shares and roughly 202 million ordinary shares outstanding, the correct ADS-equivalent share count is about 67.3 million, implying a market cap near $66 million, not the inflated $200 million figure some data feeds show when they mishandle the ADS ratio.

The core fact is simple: Viomi’s 2025 20-F shows RMB2.43 billion of revenue, up 14.6%, and RMB141.6 million of net income attributable to ordinary shareholders, or about $20.3 million. Non-GAAP net income was $22.3 million in the March 2026 results release. At today’s price, VIOT trades around 3.3x GAAP earnings and 3.0x non-GAAP earnings.

The balance sheet is the real torque. At year-end 2025, Viomi had $115.3 million of cash, $36.9 million of short-term deposits, $11.8 million of short-term investments, and $23.5 million of restricted cash. Total liabilities were $122.8 million. That means unrestricted cash, deposits, and investments exceed interest-bearing debt by a wide margin, and liquid assets after all liabilities are roughly the entire market cap. Book equity attributable to shareholders was $218.3 million, or about $3.24 per ADS. The stock trades near 0.30x book and below half of net current asset value.

The variant perception is that investors are looking at second-half weakness and China ADR governance risk, then writing off the whole business. That is directionally understandable but too punitive. Second-half 2025 revenue fell 25.9% year over year and net income fell 70.2%, largely because the China home-appliance trade-in subsidy faded and Viomi kept spending on overseas expansion, product development, and brand building. The market is treating that H2 run-rate as the new normal. The better base case is that 2025 was a subsidy-distorted year, 2026 is an investment year, and normalized earnings power is still somewhere around $0.22 to $0.35 per ADS unless the Xiaomi relationship breaks.

The Xiaomi relationship is both the thesis and the risk. Xiaomi represented about 90% of 2025 revenue, and Xiaomi’s strategic investment executive Qian Sun joined Viomi’s board in February 2026. This is not a standalone Western-style branded appliance compounder. It is a tiny, listed supplier plugged into Xiaomi’s ecosystem. That dependency deserves a discount, but not a liquidation multiple while the company remains profitable, cash-rich, and shareholder-return oriented.

Capital allocation is unusually supportive for a China micro-cap. Viomi paid a $0.088 per ADS special dividend in 2025 and declared another $0.066 per ADS special dividend in March 2026. Together, that is $0.154 per ADS, or nearly 16% of today’s stock price. The board also authorized a $20 million ADS repurchase program through 2027 and had already bought 1.02 million ADSs for $2.5 million by year-end 2025. At the current price, the remaining authorization could theoretically retire more than a quarter of the ADS count, though liquidity will limit the pace.

The upside option is overseas water. Management says North American Amazon sales grew triple digits sequentially in H2 2025, with its products ranking 19th in water purifiers and fourth in under-sink tankless RO during Black Friday. It is also pushing Malaysia, Europe, WQA, and new overseas-ready production lines expected to enter mass production in Q2 2026. This is still small-base evidence, not proof. But a $66 million market cap does not require overseas success. It only requires that the core business does not collapse and that management keeps returning capital.

Retail context is almost ideal for a mispricing thesis. VIOT has roughly 1,400 Stocktwits watchers, sparse Reddit activity mostly from automated news bots, minimal short interest, low institutional ownership, and essentially no active sponsorship. It is too small and too illiquid for most funds, too Chinese for value screens, too boring for retail momentum, and too messy for clean quant factor ownership.

The bear case is real. Xiaomi concentration is extreme. Consumables revenue fell 15.2% in 2025, which weakens the simple razor-and-blade story. China subsidy pull-forward may have flattered first-half 2025 demand. Governance is founder and Xiaomi controlled through dual-class shares. The company can stop buying stock, waste cash, or remain permanently ignored. As an ADR, it also carries China audit, political, and liquidity discounts.

But the stock is already priced for a lot of that. A profitable company with $347 million of revenue, $20 million of net income, $218 million of book equity, real dividends, and an active buyback is trading at $66 million. Even if earnings fall by half in 2026, the stock is still only 6 to 7x depressed earnings before giving credit for the balance sheet.

Bear target: $0.55, down 44% from the current $0.98. This assumes H2 weakness becomes the real run-rate, Xiaomi orders deteriorate, capital returns slow, and the market keeps applying a deep China micro-cap discount.

Base target: $2.50, up 155%. This is roughly 0.75x book and 7 to 9x normalized earnings of $0.28 to $0.35 per ADS, with buybacks and special dividends continuing.

Bull target: $5.00, up 409%. This assumes the Xiaomi relationship holds, overseas water products begin contributing visible growth, earnings stay around $0.35 to $0.45 per ADS, and the stock rerates to a still-modest 11 to 14x earnings or 1.5x book.
